description: Ground truth Q&A for BigMem eval. Questions reference facts seeded from alli_flash.

questions.question: What are the 5 most common CNV syndromes found in prenatal diagnosis?
questions.expected_answer: DiGeorge syndrome (22q11.2 deletion), 1p36 deletion syndrome, Prader-Willi syndrome (15q11.2-q13 paternal deletion), Angelman syndrome (15q11.2-q13 maternal deletion), Cri du chat syndrome (5p deletion). CNVs are detected by chromosomal microarray (CMA), not standard karyotype.

questions.question: What is the biophysical profile (BPP) and how are the scores interpreted?
questions.expected_answer: The BPP has five variables: fetal breathing movements, gross body movements, fetal tone, amniotic fluid volume, and nonstress test. Each scored 0 or 2. Score 8-10 is normal, 6/10 is equivocal (may require repeat testing or delivery depending on context), 4 or less suggests fetal compromise.

questions.question: What is the difference between meiosis I and meiosis II errors, and how does maternal age affect this?
questions.expected_answer: Meiosis I separates homologous chromosomes (4n to 2n) and errors cause heterodisomy. Meiosis II separates sister chromatids (2n to n) and errors cause isodisomy. Advanced maternal age increases nondisjunction risk because oogenesis arrests in prophase I from 8 months gestation until ovulation, meaning decades of arrest lead to increased errors.

questions.question: What is the discriminatory zone for hCG?
questions.expected_answer: The discriminatory zone is the hCG level (1,100-1,500 mU/mL) above which an intrauterine pregnancy should be visible on endovaginal ultrasound. If no gestational sac is visible above this level, it suggests abnormal gestation or ectopic pregnancy.

questions.question: How is CPAM volume ratio (CVR) calculated and what are the prognostic implications?
questions.expected_answer: CVR is calculated as (length x height x width x 0.52) divided by head circumference. CVR greater than 1.6 is associated with increased risk of hydrops. CPAM with high CVR may require fetal intervention.

questions.question: What risk factors and screening methods are used to predict spontaneous preterm birth?
questions.expected_answer: The strongest individual risk factor is prior spontaneous preterm birth. Short cervical length (25mm or less at 22-24 weeks) is a strong predictor. Fetal fibronectin (fFN) has high negative predictive value for ruling out preterm delivery within 7-14 days.

questions.question: What are the clinical features of Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and what is the genetic mechanism?
questions.expected_answer: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome features overgrowth, macroglossia, and omphalocele. The genetic mechanism involves paternal UPD of 11p15 or 11p15 methylation abnormalities. It is an imprinting disorder.

questions.question: What is Eisenmenger syndrome and why is it particularly dangerous in pregnancy?
questions.expected_answer: Eisenmenger syndrome is the reversal of a left-to-right shunt to right-to-left due to pulmonary hypertension. It is extremely dangerous in pregnancy with very high maternal mortality. The hemodynamic changes of pregnancy (decreased SVR, increased blood volume) can worsen the right-to-left shunting.
